standard.notes: A key landmark word when giving or following directions.
Regular -er verb. Essential for giving directions.
examples: Tournez à droite après le pont.

standard.notes: Refers to a town square or public plaza, not a seat (which is also 'place' in context).
examples: La place du marché est au centre-ville.
The market square is in the town centre.
Traversez le pont et continuez tout droit.
Cross the bridge and continue straight ahead.

standard.notes: French pharmacies are marked by a green cross sign. Very easy to spot.
examples: Il y a une pharmacie près d'ici ?
Is there a pharmacy near here?
La pharmacie est en face de la boulangerie.
The pharmacy is opposite the bakery.

advanced.notes: A key landmark word when giving or following directions.
Regular -er verb. Essential for giving directions.
examples: Tournez à droite après le pont.

advanced.notes: Refers to a town square or public plaza, not a seat (which is also 'place' in context).
examples: La place du marché est au centre-ville.
The market square is in the town centre.
Traversez le pont et continuez tout droit.
Cross the bridge and continue straight ahead.

advanced.notes: French pharmacies are marked by a green cross sign. Very easy to spot.
examples: Il y a une pharmacie près d'ici ?
Is there a pharmacy near here?
La pharmacie est en face de la boulangerie.
The pharmacy is opposite the bakery.

advanced.notes: France has more roundabouts per capita than almost any other country. Knowing this word is very practical.
examples: Prenez la deuxième sortie au rond-point.
Take the second exit at the roundabout.
Le rond-point est à cinq minutes d'ici.
The roundabout is five minutes from here.

advanced.notes: Short for 'le feu de signalisation'. In directions, 'au feu' means 'at the traffic lights'.
examples: Turn right at the traffic lights.

dialogue: Excusez-moi, je cherche la gare. Vous pouvez m'aider ?
Bien sûr ! La gare n'est pas loin d'ici.
Super ! Je dois tourner à droite ou à gauche ?
Tournez à gauche, traversez le pont, et la gare est sur votre droite.
Non, c'est environ dix minutes à pied. Bonne route !

questions.question: Qu'est-ce que le touriste cherche ?
questions.explanation: The tourist says 'je cherche la gare' — I am looking for the train station.

questions.question: Qu'est-ce que le touriste doit traverser ?
questions.explanation: The local says 'traversez le pont' — cross the bridge.

questions.question: Combien de temps à pied jusqu'à la gare ?
questions.explanation: The local says 'c'est environ dix minutes à pied' — it's about ten minutes on foot.

standard.summary: A tourist asks a local for directions to the train station. The local explains to go straight ahead to the crossroads, turn left, cross the bridge, and the station will be on the right — about ten minutes on foot.

dialogue: Pardon, madame. Est-ce qu'il y a une pharmacie dans le quartier ?
Oui, il y en a une rue Voltaire. Ce n'est pas compliqué.
Pouvez-vous m'expliquer le chemin, s'il vous plaît ?
Bien sûr. Prenez cette rue jusqu'au rond-point, puis prenez la première sortie.
La première sortie au rond-point — d'accord.
Ensuite, au feu rouge, tournez à droite. Vous verrez la place Saint-Michel devant vous.
Et la pharmacie est sur la place ?
Non, continuez sur la gauche de la place et la pharmacie est à cinquante mètres, entre la boulangerie et le café.

questions.question: Où se trouve la pharmacie ?
questions.explanation: The local specifies 'il y en a une rue Voltaire' — there is one on Rue Voltaire.

questions.question: Que faut-il faire au rond-point ?
questions.explanation: The local says 'prenez la première sortie' at the roundabout.

questions.question: Où est la pharmacie par rapport à la place Saint-Michel ?
options: À gauche de la place, à cinquante mètres
questions.correctAnswer: À gauche de la place, à cinquante mètres
questions.explanation: The local says to continue on the left side of the square — the pharmacy is fifty metres along, between the bakery and the café.

advanced.summary: A tourist asks a local how to find a pharmacy. The local gives detailed directions: take the street to the roundabout, take the first exit, turn right at the red light, pass the Place Saint-Michel, then continue to the left of the square where the pharmacy is located fifty metres along.

standard.title: Question Formation (est-ce que, intonation, question words)
standard.explanation:
In French, there are three main ways to ask a question.

**1. Rising intonation** — Take a statement and raise your voice at the end. This is the most casual and common in speech.
• Vous avez une pharmacie ? (You have a pharmacy?)

**2. Est-ce que** — Place 'est-ce que' before a regular statement. This works in any situation and is very natural.
• Est-ce que vous avez une pharmacie ? (Do you have a pharmacy?)

**3. Question words** — Use a question word to ask for specific information. The most useful ones are:
• où (where) — Où est la gare ?
• quand (when) — Quand est-ce que le bus arrive ?
• comment (how) — Comment est-ce que je peux aller à la place ?
• pourquoi (why) — Pourquoi est-ce que le pont est fermé ?
• qu'est-ce que (what) — Qu'est-ce que vous cherchez ?
• combien (how much/many) — Combien de minutes à pied ?

Question words can be combined with est-ce que for a complete, natural-sounding question.

examples.french: Comment est-ce que je peux aller à la gare ?
examples.english: How can I get to the train station?

drills.question: ___-ce que vous pouvez m'aider ? (Can you help me?)
drills.explanation: 'Est-ce que' is placed at the start of a question to turn a statement into a question without changing word order.

drills.question: Which question word means 'where'?
drills.explanation: 'Où' means 'where'. For example: Où est la gare ? (Where is the train station?)

drills.question: ___ est le prochain carrefour ? (Where is the next crossroads?)
drills.explanation: 'Où' asks about location. It goes at the beginning of the question.

drills.question: How do you ask 'How many minutes on foot?' in French?
drills.explanation: 'Combien' means 'how much' or 'how many'. It is followed by 'de' before a noun.

drills.question: Which sentence is a correct question using est-ce que?
options: Est-ce que le pont est loin ?
drills.correctAnswer: Est-ce que le pont est loin ?
drills.explanation: 'Est-ce que' goes at the very beginning, followed by the normal statement word order: le pont est loin.

advanced.title: Question Formation — Est-ce que, Inversion & Question Words
advanced.explanation:
French has three question-forming methods, each suited to a different register.

**1. Rising intonation** — Informal speech only. Keep statement word order, raise pitch at the end.
• Vous habitez ici ? (You live here?)

**2. Est-ce que** — Neutral register, works in speech and writing. Place est-ce que before the subject + verb.
• Est-ce que vous habitez ici ?

**3. Inversion** — Formal register, written French, and official speech. Invert the subject pronoun and verb with a hyphen.
• Habitez-vous ici ?
With a third-person singular verb ending in a vowel, insert -t- to aid pronunciation:
• Parle-t-il français ? (Does he speak French?)
• Où va-t-elle ? (Where is she going?)

**Extended question words with est-ce que or inversion:**
• Pourquoi est-ce que le carrefour est fermé ? / Pourquoi le carrefour est-il fermé ?
• À quelle heure est-ce que le train part ? / À quelle heure le train part-il ?
• De où venez-vous ? → D'où venez-vous ? (Where are you from?)

Note: 'Qu'est-ce que' is already a fixed expression and does not combine with further inversion in everyday French.

examples.english: Why is the bridge closed? (formal inversion)

examples.french: À quelle heure est-ce que la pharmacie ouvre ?
examples.english: What time does the pharmacy open?

drills.question: Parle-___-il français ? (Does he speak French? — insert the linking letter)
drills.explanation: When a third-person singular verb ends in a vowel and the subject is 'il', 'elle', or 'on', insert '-t-' between the verb and subject pronoun to avoid hiatus (two vowel sounds clashing).

drills.question: Which is the formal (inversion) form of 'Est-ce que vous cherchez la gare ?'
options: Qu'est-ce que vous cherchez la gare ?
drills.explanation: Inversion places the verb first, then adds the subject pronoun with a hyphen: Cherchez-vous…?

drills.question: D'___ venez-vous ? (Where are you from?)
drills.explanation: 'D'où' is the contracted form of 'de où'. In French, 'de' and 'où' contract to 'd'où' when asking about origin.

drills.question: How do you say 'Why is the pharmacy closed?' using inversion?
options: Pourquoi est-ce que fermée la pharmacie ?
drills.explanation: When the subject is a noun (not a pronoun), you keep the noun, then add the matching pronoun after the verb with a hyphen: la pharmacie est-elle…?

drills.question: Which question asks 'What time does the train leave?' correctly?
options: À quelle heure est-ce que le train part ?
Quand est-ce que part le train à heure ?
Quelle part le train est-ce que ?
drills.correctAnswer: À quelle heure est-ce que le train part ?
drills.explanation: 'À quelle heure' means 'at what time'. It combines naturally with est-ce que followed by normal statement word order.

drills.question: Où ___ -t-il habiter ? (Where does he want to live? — verb: veut)
drills.explanation: The inversion form is: question word + verb + -t- (because 'veut' ends in a vowel) + pronoun. Result: Où veut-il habiter ?

drills.question: Which sentence correctly uses inversion with a noun subject?
options: Quand est-ce que arrive-t-il le bus ?
drills.explanation: With a noun subject, keep the noun before the verb, then add the pronoun after: le bus arrive-t-il? The -t- is needed because 'arrive' ends in a vowel.

drills.question: Combien de temps ___ -il pour aller à la gare ? (How long does it take to get to the station? — verb: faut)
drills.explanation: 'Combien de temps faut-il ?' is a fixed, very useful expression meaning 'how long does it take?'. 'Faut' comes from the impersonal verb 'falloir'.

standard.question: What does 'le pont' mean?
standard.explanation: 'Le pont' means 'the bridge'. Example: Traversez le pont — Cross the bridge.

standard.question: Complete the direction: Tournez à gauche au ___. (Turn left at the crossroads.)
standard.explanation: 'Le carrefour' means 'the crossroads' or 'the intersection'. It is a masculine noun.

standard.question: How do you say 'Where is the pharmacy?' in French?
standard.explanation: 'Où' means 'where'. 'Où est la pharmacie ?' — Where is the pharmacy?

standard.question: ___-ce que vous pouvez répéter ? (Can you repeat that?)
standard.explanation: 'Est-ce que' is used to form a question without changing the word order of the statement.

standard.question: A tourist says: 'Je cherche la place.' What are they looking for?
standard.explanation: 'La place' means 'the square' or 'the plaza' — a public open space in a town.

advanced.question: What does 'le rond-point' mean?
advanced.explanation: 'Le rond-point' means 'the roundabout'. Example: Prenez la deuxième sortie au rond-point.

advanced.question: Tournez à droite au ___. (Turn right at the traffic lights.)
advanced.explanation: 'Le feu' (short for 'feu de signalisation') means 'the traffic light'. 'Au feu' = at the traffic lights.

advanced.question: Which is the correct formal inversion question for 'Est-ce qu'il parle français ?'
advanced.explanation: When the verb ends in a vowel and the pronoun is 'il', insert '-t-' to aid pronunciation: Parle-t-il ?

advanced.question: Traversez le ___ et continuez tout droit. (Cross the bridge and continue straight ahead.)
How do you say 'Where are you from?' using formal inversion?
advanced.explanation: 'D'où' is the contracted form of 'de où'. With inversion: D'où venez-vous ? — Where are you from?

advanced.question: Which sentence correctly asks 'What time does the pharmacy open?' using est-ce que?
options: À quelle heure ouvre est-ce que la pharmacie ?
À quelle heure est-ce que la pharmacie ouvre ?
Est-ce que à quelle heure la pharmacie ouvre ?
La pharmacie est-ce que à quelle heure ouvre ?
advanced.correctAnswer: À quelle heure est-ce que la pharmacie ouvre ?
advanced.explanation: The question word phrase 'à quelle heure' comes first, then 'est-ce que', then the normal subject + verb order.

standard.scenario: A stranger stops you on the street and asks you how to find the nearest pharmacy. You know it is straight ahead, then turn left at the crossroads, and it is next to the bridge. Give them clear step-by-step directions in French.
keyPhrases: Continuez tout droit jusqu'au… (Continue straight ahead to the…)
Tournez à gauche / à droite (Turn left / right)
C'est à côté de… (It is next to…)
standard.modelAnswer: Continuez tout droit jusqu'au carrefour. Tournez à gauche au carrefour. La pharmacie est à côté du pont, sur votre droite.
standard.modelAnswerExplanation: This model answer uses three clear steps: go straight to the crossroads, turn left, then identify the pharmacy by its position next to the bridge. 'Sur votre droite' (on your right) is a helpful final landmark phrase.
standard.tip: When giving directions in French, use the imperative (command form): 'Continuez', 'Tournez', 'Traversez'. These are the vous-form imperatives — polite and clear for a stranger.

advanced.scenario: You are lost in a French city and need to find the train station. Stop a passerby, ask for directions politely, ask them to slow down or repeat if you don't understand, and confirm the key steps of the route before you leave.
keyPhrases: Excusez-moi, pourriez-vous m'indiquer le chemin pour… ? (Could you show me the way to…?)
Pourriez-vous répéter plus lentement, s'il vous plaît ? (Could you repeat that more slowly, please?)
Si je comprends bien, je dois… c'est bien ça ? (If I understand correctly, I need to… is that right?)
Prenez la sortie / le feu / le rond-point (Take the exit / the traffic light / the roundabout)
advanced.modelAnswer: Excusez-moi, madame. Pourriez-vous m'indiquer le chemin pour aller à la gare ? Je suis un peu perdu. … Pardon, pourriez-vous répéter plus lentement, s'il vous plaît ? … Alors, si je comprends bien, je dois continuer jusqu'au rond-point, prendre la deuxième sortie, tourner à droite au feu, traverser le pont, et la gare est sur la place — c'est bien ça ? Merci beaucoup, vous êtes très aimable !
advanced.modelAnswerExplanation: This model answer demonstrates a full interaction: asking politely with 'pourriez-vous' (conditional — more polite than 'pouvez-vous'), requesting clarification, summarising the route to confirm understanding, and closing with a warm thank-you. Summarising directions back to the speaker ('si je comprends bien…') is both a real-life strategy and impressive language use.
